summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Angles in Degrees & Radians
summary_sections.content_html:
Angles in Degrees & Radians

An angle is formed by two rays sharing a common endpoint (vertex). Angles can be measured in degrees or radians.

Degrees

  • A full rotation = 360°.
  • A straight angle = 180°; a right angle = 90°.

Radians

  • A radian measures the angle subtended by an arc equal in length to the radius.
  • A full rotation = 2π radians ≈ 6.283 rad.
  • π radians = 180°.

Conversion Formulas

  • Degrees → Radians: multiply by π/180.
  • Radians → Degrees: multiply by 180/π.

Example: 90° × π/180 = π/2 rad. Example: π/3 rad × 180/π = 60°.

flashcards.definition: A unit of angle measure where a full rotation equals 360°.
A unit of angle measure where the angle is subtended by an arc equal in length to the radius; a full rotation = 2π radians.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Arc Length & Sector Area
summary_sections.content_html:
Arc Length & Sector Area

When an angle is measured in radians, there are elegant formulas connecting the angle to the arc and sector of a circle.

Arc Length

  • s = rθ where s = arc length, r = radius, θ = central angle in radians.

Sector Area

  • A = ½r²θ where A = area of the sector, r = radius, θ = central angle in radians.

Note: If the angle is in degrees, first convert to radians or use: s = (θ/360)·2πr and A = (θ/360)·πr².

The relationship s = rθ shows why radians are natural: arc length is simply radius × angle.

A = ½r²θ, where A is the sector area, r is the radius, and θ is the central angle in radians.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Right Triangle Ratios (SOH-CAH-TOA)
summary_sections.content_html:
Right Triangle Ratios (SOH-CAH-TOA)

In a right triangle, the three primary trigonometric ratios relate the sides to an acute angle θ:

  • sin θ = opposite / hypotenuse
  • cos θ = adjacent / hypotenuse
  • tan θ = opposite / adjacent

The mnemonic SOH-CAH-TOA helps remember these:

  • Sine = Opposite / Hypotenuse
  • Cosine = Adjacent / Hypotenuse
  • Tangent = Opposite / Adjacent

These ratios depend only on the angle, not on the size of the triangle (since all right triangles with the same acute angle are similar).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Special Triangles (30°–60°–90°, 45°–45°–90°)
summary_sections.content_html:
Special Triangles

Two right triangles have exact, memorizable side ratios:

45°–45°–90° Triangle

  • Both legs are equal: ratio 1 : 1 : √2.
  • If each leg = a, then hypotenuse = a√2.
  • sin 45° = cos 45° = √2/2 ≈ 0.707; tan 45° = 1.

30°–60°–90° Triangle

  • Side ratio: 1 : √3 : 2 (short leg : long leg : hypotenuse).
  • Short leg is opposite 30°; long leg is opposite 60°; hypotenuse is opposite 90°.
  • sin 30° = ½, cos 30° = √3/2, tan 30° = √3/3.
  • sin 60° = √3/2, cos 60° = ½, tan 60° = √3.

These triangles come from splitting a square diagonally (45-45-90) or an equilateral triangle in half (30-60-90).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Unit Circle Basics
summary_sections.content_html:
Unit Circle Basics

The unit circle is a circle of radius 1 centered at the origin of the coordinate plane.

  • Any point on the unit circle can be written as (cos θ, sin θ) where θ is the angle from the positive x-axis.
  • The x-coordinate = cos θ, the y-coordinate = sin θ.

Key Angles on the Unit Circle

  • 0° (0): (1, 0)
  • 30° (π/6): (√3/2, 1/2)
  • 45° (π/4): (√2/2, √2/2)
  • 60° (π/3): (1/2, √3/2)
  • 90° (π/2): (0, 1)
  • 180° (π): (−1, 0)
  • 270° (3π/2): (0, −1)

The unit circle extends trig functions beyond acute angles to all real numbers.

flashcards.definition: A circle with radius 1 centered at the origin; points are (cos θ, sin θ).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Coterminal Angles & Reference Angles
summary_sections.content_html:
Coterminal Angles & Reference Angles

Coterminal Angles

  • Angles that share the same terminal side are coterminal.
  • Add or subtract 360° (or 2π) to find coterminal angles: θ ± 360°n.
  • Example: 30° and 390° are coterminal (390 − 360 = 30).

Reference Angles

  • The reference angle is the acute angle between the terminal side and the x-axis.
  • Q I: ref = θ. Q II: ref = 180° − θ. Q III: ref = θ − 180°. Q IV: ref = 360° − θ.
  • Trig values of any angle can be found using its reference angle and the sign for that quadrant.

ASTC rule: All trig functions positive in Q I; Sine in Q II; Tangent in Q III; Cosine in Q IV.

flashcards.definition: Angles that share the same terminal side; found by adding or subtracting multiples of 360° (or 2π).
The acute angle between the terminal side and the x-axis; always between 0° and 90°.
All (Q I), Sine (Q II), Tangent (Q III), Cosine (Q IV) — tells which trig functions are positive in each quadrant.
An angle measured clockwise from the positive x-axis.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Applications in Geometry
summary_sections.content_html:
Applications of Trigonometry in Geometry

Trigonometric ratios and radian measure have wide applications in geometry:

Finding Lengths and Angles

  • Use right-triangle trig to find unknown sides or angles in any geometric figure that contains right triangles.
  • A diagonal of a rectangle creates two right triangles — trig finds the diagonal length or angle.

Area Using Trig

  • Area of a triangle: A = ½ab sin C (two sides and the included angle).
  • Area of a sector: A = ½r²θ (central angle in radians).

Regular Polygons

  • For a regular n-gon inscribed in a circle of radius r, the side length = 2r sin(π/n) and the area = ½nr² sin(2π/n).

Trigonometry bridges the gap between angle measures and linear/area measures throughout geometry.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Definition of Sine, Cosine, Tangent
summary_sections.content_html:
Definition of Sine, Cosine, Tangent

Using the unit circle, trig functions are defined for all real numbers, not just acute angles.

Unit-Circle Definitions

  • sin θ = y-coordinate of the point on the unit circle.
  • cos θ = x-coordinate of the point on the unit circle.
  • tan θ = sin θ / cos θ = y/x (undefined when cos θ = 0).

Domain & Range

  • sin θ: domain = all reals, range = [−1, 1].
  • cos θ: domain = all reals, range = [−1, 1].
  • tan θ: domain = all reals except θ = π/2 + nπ, range = (−∞, ∞).

These definitions are consistent with SOH-CAH-TOA for acute angles but extend to all quadrants.

The x-coordinate of the point where the terminal side meets the unit circle.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Reciprocal Functions (csc, sec, cot)
summary_sections.content_html:
Reciprocal Trigonometric Functions

Three additional trig functions are defined as reciprocals of the primary three:

  • csc θ = 1/sin θ (cosecant) — undefined when sin θ = 0.
  • sec θ = 1/cos θ (secant) — undefined when cos θ = 0.
  • cot θ = 1/tan θ = cos θ/sin θ (cotangent) — undefined when sin θ = 0.

Domains

  • csc θ: all reals except nπ.
  • sec θ: all reals except π/2 + nπ.
  • cot θ: all reals except nπ.

Ranges

  • csc θ and sec θ: (−∞, −1] ∪ [1, ∞).
  • cot θ: (−∞, ∞).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Graphs of Sine & Cosine
summary_sections.content_html:
Graphs of Sine & Cosine

The Sine Graph: y = sin x

  • Starts at (0, 0), rises to 1 at π/2, returns to 0 at π, drops to −1 at 3π/2, returns to 0 at 2π.
  • Period: 2π (one full cycle). Amplitude: 1. Midline: y = 0.

The Cosine Graph: y = cos x

  • Starts at (0, 1), drops to 0 at π/2, reaches −1 at π, returns to 0 at 3π/2, back to 1 at 2π.
  • Same period, amplitude, and midline as sine.

cos x is a phase-shifted sine: cos x = sin(x + π/2).

Both graphs are smooth, continuous waves called sinusoidal curves.

flashcards.definition: The length of one full cycle; for y = sin x and y = cos x, period = 2π.
Half the distance between max and min values; for basic sin/cos, amplitude = 1.
The horizontal line halfway between max and min; for basic sin/cos, midline = y = 0.
Having the shape of a sine or cosine wave.
A horizontal translation; cos x = sin(x + π/2) is sine shifted left by π/2.

summary_sections.content_html:
Graphs of the Other Four Trig Functions

y = tan x

  • Period = π. Vertical asymptotes at x = π/2 + nπ (where cos x = 0).
  • Passes through the origin. Increasing throughout each period. Range = (−∞, ∞).

y = cot x

  • Decreasing throughout each period.

u2_l2.5.title: Transformations of Trig Graphs (amplitude, period, phase shift)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Transformations of Trig Graphs (amplitude, period, phase shift)
summary_sections.content_html:
Transformations of Trig Graphs

The general sinusoidal form is y = A sin(Bx − C) + D (or cosine).

Parameters

  • A (amplitude): |A| = vertical stretch/compression. If A < 0, the graph is reflected over the x-axis.
  • B (period change): Period = 2π/|B|. Larger |B| → shorter period.
  • C/B (phase shift): Horizontal shift = C/B to the right.
  • D (vertical shift): The midline moves to y = D.

Example: y = 3 sin(2x − π) + 1 has amplitude 3, period π, phase shift π/2 right, midline y = 1.

flashcards.definition: |A| in y = A sin(Bx − C) + D; the maximum displacement from the midline.
2π/|B|; the horizontal length of one complete cycle.
C/B; the horizontal shift of the graph (right if positive).
Moves the midline from y = 0 to y = D.
If A < 0, the graph is flipped over the x-axis.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Symmetry & Periodicity
summary_sections.content_html:
Symmetry & Periodicity

Even and Odd Functions

  • Cosine is even: cos(−θ) = cos θ. Graph is symmetric about the y-axis.
  • Sine is odd: sin(−θ) = −sin θ. Graph is symmetric about the origin.
  • Tangent is odd: tan(−θ) = −tan θ.

Periodicity

  • sin and cos have period 2π: f(θ + 2π) = f(θ).
  • tan and cot have period π: f(θ + π) = f(θ).
  • Periodicity means you only need one cycle to know all values.

Symmetry and periodicity together reduce calculations: use reference angles and sign rules.

flashcards.definition: f(−x) = f(x); symmetric about the y-axis. Cosine is even.
f(−x) = −f(x); symmetric about the origin. Sine and tangent are odd.
The property that a function repeats at regular intervals: f(x + P) = f(x).
2π — the smallest interval after which the function repeats.
π — tangent and cotangent repeat every π radians.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Applications in Sound Waves
summary_sections.content_html:
Applications in Sound Waves

Sound waves are modeled by sinusoidal functions because they involve periodic oscillations of air pressure.

Modeling Sound

  • A basic sound wave: y = A sin(2πft) where A = amplitude (loudness), f = frequency (pitch), t = time.
  • Frequency is measured in hertz (Hz): cycles per second. Higher frequency → higher pitch.
  • Period = 1/f: time for one complete cycle.

Musical Concepts

  • Standard tuning: A4 = 440 Hz.
  • An octave doubles the frequency: A5 = 880 Hz.
  • Overlapping sound waves create beats — periodic fluctuations in loudness.

Understanding sinusoidal functions helps in acoustics, audio engineering, and music theory.

flashcards.definition: Number of complete cycles per second; measured in hertz (Hz).
The maximum pressure variation; corresponds to loudness or volume.
T = 1/f; the time for one complete vibration cycle.
Unit of frequency: 1 Hz = 1 cycle per second.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Definition & Domains of Inverse Trig Functions
summary_sections.content_html:
Inverse Trigonometric Functions

Because trig functions are periodic (not one-to-one), we restrict domains to create invertible functions.

Principal Restrictions

  • sin⁻¹ (arcsin): domain [−1, 1], range [−π/2, π/2].
  • cos⁻¹ (arccos): domain [−1, 1], range [0, π].
  • tan⁻¹ (arctan): domain (−∞, ∞), range (−π/2, π/2).

Inverse trig functions answer the question: "What angle has this trig value?"

Notation: sin⁻¹ x = arcsin x (not the reciprocal 1/sin x).

flashcards.definition: Inverse sine; domain [−1, 1], range [−π/2, π/2].
Inverse cosine; domain [−1, 1], range [0, π].
Inverse tangent; domain (−∞, ∞), range (−π/2, π/2).
The unique output of an inverse trig function within its restricted range.
Limiting the domain of a trig function so it becomes one-to-one and thus invertible.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Solving Equations with Inverse Trig
summary_sections.content_html:
Solving Equations with Inverse Trig Functions

To solve equations like sin θ = 0.6, use inverse trig functions to find θ.

Steps

  • Step 1: Isolate the trig function.
  • Step 2: Apply the inverse to get the principal value.
  • Step 3: Find all solutions using symmetry and periodicity.

Example

Solve sin θ = 0.5 for 0 ≤ θ ≤ 2π.

  • Principal: θ₁ = arcsin(0.5) = π/6.
  • Sine is also positive in Q II: θ₂ = π − π/6 = 5π/6.
  • Solutions: θ = π/6, 5π/6.

flashcards.definition: The first solution returned by an inverse trig function within its restricted range.
If sin θ = a, then θ = arcsin(a) + 2nπ or θ = π − arcsin(a) + 2nπ.
If cos θ = a, then θ = ±arccos(a) + 2nπ.
If tan θ = a, then θ = arctan(a) + nπ.
Rearrange the equation so the trig function is alone on one side before applying the inverse.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Applications in Geometry & Physics
summary_sections.content_html:
Applications of Inverse Trig in Geometry & Physics

Geometry

  • Finding angles in right triangles: θ = arctan(opposite/adjacent).
  • Angle of elevation/depression: using trig ratios and inverses.
  • Angle between vectors: θ = arccos(u·v / (|u||v|)).

Physics

  • Projectile motion: launch angle θ = arctan(v_y / v_x).
  • Inclined planes: component analysis using trig inverses.
  • Optics: Snell's law → θ₂ = arcsin((n₁ sin θ₁)/n₂).

flashcards.definition: The angle above the horizontal line of sight, found with arctan(height/distance).
The angle below the horizontal line of sight to an object below the observer.

n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂; solve for θ₂ using arcsin.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Restrictions & Principal Values
summary_sections.content_html:
Restrictions & Principal Values

Because trig functions map many angles to the same value, inverse trig functions must use restricted ranges.

Key Identities for Composition

  • sin(arcsin x) = x for x ∈ [−1, 1].
  • arcsin(sin θ) = θ only if θ ∈ [−π/2, π/2].
  • cos(arccos x) = x for x ∈ [−1, 1].
  • arccos(cos θ) = θ only if θ ∈ [0, π].

Common Pitfall

arcsin(sin(5π/6)) ≠ 5π/6 because 5π/6 ∉ [−π/2, π/2]. Answer: arcsin(1/2) = π/6.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Applications in Engineering
summary_sections.content_html:
Engineering Applications of Inverse Trig

Signal Processing

  • Phase detection: arctan(Im/Re) extracts the phase angle of a complex signal.
  • Beamforming: arcsin(d sin θ / λ) determines antenna array steering angles.

Mechanical Engineering

  • Linkage analysis: inverse trig finds joint angles from link lengths.
  • Cam design: follower position involves arctan and arccos of geometric ratios.

Electrical Engineering

  • Power factor angle: θ = arccos(P/S) where P = real power, S = apparent power.
  • Impedance angle: θ = arctan(X_L / R) for an RL circuit.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Pythagorean Identities
summary_sections.content_html:
Pythagorean Identities

Derived from sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 (the unit circle equation x² + y² = 1).

Three Forms

  • sin²θ + cos²θ = 1
  • Divide by cos²θ: tan²θ + 1 = sec²θ
  • Divide by sin²θ: 1 + cot²θ = csc²θ

These identities are used to simplify expressions, verify other identities, and solve equations.

flashcards.definition: The fundamental Pythagorean identity, true for all θ.
Obtained by dividing sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 by cos²θ.
Obtained by dividing sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 by sin²θ.
On the unit circle, x = cos θ, y = sin θ, and x² + y² = 1 gives sin² + cos² = 1.
Replace one trig function with another to simplify: e.g., sin²θ = 1 − cos²θ.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Reciprocal Identities
summary_sections.content_html:
Reciprocal Identities

Each trig function has a reciprocal partner:

  • csc θ = 1/sin θ and sin θ = 1/csc θ
  • sec θ = 1/cos θ and cos θ = 1/sec θ
  • cot θ = 1/tan θ and tan θ = 1/cot θ

These identities let you convert between any trig function and its reciprocal to simplify expressions.

Usage

Replace less common functions (csc, sec, cot) with familiar ones (sin, cos, tan) when simplifying or verifying identities.

flashcards.definition: Cosecant is the reciprocal of sine.
Secant is the reciprocal of cosine.
Cotangent is the reciprocal of tangent.
Two functions f and g such that f(θ) · g(θ) = 1 (e.g., sin · csc = 1).
Rewrite csc, sec, cot in terms of sin and cos, then simplify.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Quotient Identities
summary_sections.content_html:
Quotient Identities

  • tan θ = sin θ / cos θ
  • cot θ = cos θ / sin θ

These are the two quotient identities. They connect tangent and cotangent to sine and cosine.

Applications

  • Rewrite complicated expressions in terms of sin and cos using the quotient identities.
  • Combined with reciprocal and Pythagorean identities, quotient identities are powerful simplification tools.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Cofunction Identities
summary_sections.content_html:
Cofunction Identities

Cofunctions of complementary angles (adding to π/2 or 90°) are equal:

  • sin(π/2 − θ) = cos θ   and   cos(π/2 − θ) = sin θ
  • tan(π/2 − θ) = cot θ   and   cot(π/2 − θ) = tan θ
  • sec(π/2 − θ) = csc θ   and   csc(π/2 − θ) = sec θ

The prefix "co-" means complement: co-sine, co-tangent, co-secant.

flashcards.definition: A pair of trig functions whose values swap at complementary angles: sin/cos, tan/cot, sec/csc.
Two angles that sum to 90° (or π/2 radians).
The sine of the complement equals the cosine.
The tangent of the complement equals the cotangent.
The secant of the complement equals the cosecant.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Verifying Identities
summary_sections.content_html:
Verifying Trig Identities

To verify (prove) an identity, transform one side until it equals the other. Key techniques:

  • Work on the more complex side.
  • Convert everything to sin and cos.
  • Factor, combine fractions, or multiply by a conjugate.
  • Use Pythagorean, reciprocal, quotient, and cofunction identities.

Important: Never cross-multiply or treat it as an equation — you're proving, not solving.

flashcards.definition: Showing that one side of the equation can be transformed into the other using known identities.
Start with the more complicated side — it's easier to simplify than to build up.
Rewriting all trig functions as sin and cos often reveals simplifications.
Multiply by (1 + sin θ)/(1 + sin θ) or similar to create difference-of-squares patterns.
Identities are proven by transforming one side; treating both sides equally assumes the result.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Simplifying Expressions – Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Simplifying Trig Expressions — Applications

Combining Pythagorean, reciprocal, quotient, and cofunction identities to simplify real-world and mathematical expressions.

Strategy Summary

  • 1. Convert to sin/cos.
  • 2. Apply Pythagorean identities.
  • 3. Factor or combine fractions.
  • 4. Cancel common factors.

Simplified expressions are easier to evaluate, differentiate, and integrate — essential skills for calculus.

flashcards.definition: Replace csc, sec, tan, cot with sin and cos expressions.
Use sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 or variants to reduce terms.
Factor common terms or get a common denominator for fractions.
Cancel common factors in numerator and denominator.
Reach a simpler equivalent expression — fewer terms, simpler functions.

Wave Superposition

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Sum & Difference Formulas for Sine & Cosine
summary_sections.content_html:
Sum & Difference Formulas

Sine

  • sin(A + B) = sin A cos B + cos A sin B
  • sin(A − B) = sin A cos B − cos A sin B

Cosine

  • cos(A + B) = cos A cos B − sin A sin B
  • cos(A − B) = cos A cos B + sin A sin B

These formulas let you compute exact values for non-standard angles: e.g., sin 75° = sin(45° + 30°).

Half-Angle Formulas

Derived from the double-angle cosine formulas by replacing A with A/2:

  • sin(A/2) = ±√((1 − cos A)/2)
  • cos(A/2) = ±√((1 + cos A)/2)
  • tan(A/2) = sin A/(1 + cos A) = (1 − cos A)/sin A

The ± sign depends on the quadrant of A/2.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Product-to-Sum & Sum-to-Product Formulas
summary_sections.content_html:
Product-to-Sum & Sum-to-Product Formulas

Product-to-Sum

  • sin A cos B = ½[sin(A+B) + sin(A−B)]
  • cos A sin B = ½[sin(A+B) − sin(A−B)]
  • cos A cos B = ½[cos(A−B) + cos(A+B)]
  • sin A sin B = ½[cos(A−B) − cos(A+B)]

Sum-to-Product

  • sin A + sin B = 2 sin((A+B)/2) cos((A−B)/2)
  • sin A − sin B = 2 cos((A+B)/2) sin((A−B)/2)
  • cos A + cos B = 2 cos((A+B)/2) cos((A−B)/2)
  • cos A − cos B = −2 sin((A+B)/2) sin((A−B)/2)

flashcards.definition: sin A cos B = ½[sin(A+B) + sin(A−B)].
cos A cos B = ½[cos(A−B) + cos(A+B)].

cos A + cos B = 2 cos((A+B)/2) cos((A−B)/2).
Product-to-sum simplifies integration; sum-to-product helps solve equations.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Engineering Cases
summary_sections.content_html:
Engineering Applications of Sum/Double/Half-Angle Formulas

Electrical Engineering

  • AC power: P(t) = V₀I₀ sin(ωt) sin(ωt+φ). Use product-to-sum → P = (V₀I₀/2)[cos φ − cos(2ωt+φ)].
  • Average power = V₀I₀ cos φ / 2 (the constant term; the oscillating term averages to 0).

Mechanical Vibrations

Two vibrations at close frequencies create beats: sin ω₁t + sin ω₂t = 2 cos((ω₁−ω₂)t/2) sin((ω₁+ω₂)t/2).

Signal Processing

AM radio multiplies a carrier by a signal; frequency shifting uses product-to-sum conversions.

P_avg = V₀I₀ cosφ / 2; the oscillating term averages to zero.
Sum-to-product: sinω₁t + sinω₂t = 2cos(Δωt/2)sin(ω_avg t).

AP Calculus Connections

Many calculus problems rely on the identities from this unit:

Integration

  • ∫ sin mx cos nx dx uses product-to-sum.
  • ∫ sec²x dx = tan x + C (from tan²x + 1 = sec²x).

Limits & Derivatives

  • lim(x→0) sin x / x = 1 (used with double-angle rearrangements).
  • d/dx[sin 2x] = 2 cos 2x (chain rule + double angle).

summary_sections.content_html:
Solving Linear Trig Equations

A linear trig equation has the form a · trig(θ) + b = 0.

Steps

  • 1. Isolate the trig function.
  • 2. Use inverse trig to find the principal solution.
  • 3. Identify all solutions in the given interval using quadrant analysis.

Example

2 sin θ − 1 = 0 → sin θ = 1/2 → θ = π/6, 5π/6 (in [0, 2π)).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Solving Quadratic Trig Equations
summary_sections.content_html:
Solving Quadratic Trig Equations

Equations like 2sin²θ − sinθ − 1 = 0 are quadratic in the trig function.

Strategy

  • Let u = sin θ (or cos θ, tan θ) to get a standard quadratic: au² + bu + c = 0.
  • Factor or use the quadratic formula.
  • Solve each factor: u = k₁ or u = k₂ → trig θ = k → find θ.
  • Reject solutions outside the trig function's range.

flashcards.definition: Let u = sinθ (or cosθ) to convert to au²+bu+c = 0.
Factor the quadratic, then set each factor to zero.
u = (−b ± √(b²−4ac)) / 2a when factoring is difficult.
Discard u values outside [−1,1] for sin/cos.
Replace u with the trig function and solve for θ.

summary_sections.content_html:
Solving Multiple-Angle Equations

Equations like sin(2θ) = √3/2 or cos(3θ) = −1/2 involve a multiple of the angle.

Method

  • Let u = 2θ (or 3θ, etc.).
  • Solve for u in the expanded interval: if θ ∈ [0, 2π), then u ∈ [0, 4π) for 2θ.
  • Find all u values in the expanded interval.
  • Divide by the multiplier to get θ.

flashcards.definition: An equation where the argument of the trig function is nθ (e.g., 2θ, 3θ).
If θ ∈ [0, 2π) and argument is nθ, solve for nθ ∈ [0, 2nπ).
Simplifies the equation to a standard form in u.
After finding all u values, θ = u/n gives the final answers.
Multiple-angle equations typically have more solutions than single-angle ones in a given interval.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: General Solutions & Periodicity
summary_sections.content_html:
General Solutions & Periodicity

When no interval is specified, express all solutions using the period:

  • sin θ = k: θ = arcsin(k) + 2nπ or θ = (π − arcsin(k)) + 2nπ.
  • cos θ = k: θ = ±arccos(k) + 2nπ.
  • tan θ = k: θ = arctan(k) + nπ.

Here n is any integer. These formulas capture every solution by exploiting periodicity.

flashcards.definition: θ = arcsin(k) + 2nπ or θ = π − arcsin(k) + 2nπ, n ∈ ℤ.
θ = ±arccos(k) + 2nπ, n ∈ ℤ.
θ = arctan(k) + nπ, n ∈ ℤ (period π).
n is any integer: …, −2, −1, 0, 1, 2, …
When the domain is all reals, there are infinitely many solutions — general form captures them all.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Modeling with Trig Equations
summary_sections.content_html:
Modeling with Trig Equations

Many real-world phenomena are periodic and modeled by sinusoidal equations:

Examples

  • Temperature: T(t) = A sin(Bt − C) + D models seasonal temperature variation.
  • Tides: h(t) = A cos(Bt − C) + D models water height over time.
  • Daylight hours: periodic with a 365-day cycle.
  • Setting the model equal to a value and solving gives the time(s) when that value occurs.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Law of Sines
summary_sections.content_html:
Law of Sines

For any triangle with sides a, b, c opposite angles A, B, C:

a/sin A = b/sin B = c/sin C

When to Use

  • AAS: Two angles and a non-included side.
  • ASA: Two angles and the included side.
  • SSA: Two sides and a non-included angle (ambiguous case).

Derivation

Drop an altitude h from vertex C: h = a sin B = b sin A → a/sin A = b/sin B.

flashcards.definition: a/sin A = b/sin B = c/sin C for any triangle.
Cases where the Law of Sines is directly applicable.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: The Ambiguous Case (SSA)
summary_sections.content_html:
The Ambiguous Case (SSA)

When two sides and a non-included angle are given (SSA), there may be 0, 1, or 2 triangles.

Analysis (Given a, b, A)

  • Compute sin B = b sin A / a.
  • If sin B > 1 → no triangle.
  • If sin B = 1 → one triangle (right angle at B).
  • If sin B < 1 → B = arcsin(…) gives one acute angle; B' = 180° − B is the other. Check if A + B' < 180° → if yes, two triangles; if not, one triangle.

flashcards.definition: SSA configuration; may yield 0, 1, or 2 valid triangles.
sin B > 1 → the given side is too short to form a triangle.
sin B = 1 (right triangle) or the supplement B' makes A + B' ≥ 180°.
Both B and B' = 180° − B produce valid triangles (A + B' < 180°).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Law of Cosines
summary_sections.content_html:
Law of Cosines

c² = a² + b² − 2ab cos C

Generalizes the Pythagorean theorem (when C = 90°, cos C = 0).

When to Use

  • SAS: Two sides and the included angle → find the third side.
  • SSS: Three sides → find each angle.

Finding Angles from SSS

cos C = (a² + b² − c²) / (2ab). Then C = arccos(…).

summary_sections.content_html:
Navigation & Bearing Applications

Bearings give direction as an angle from north (clockwise). Trig laws solve real navigation problems.

Typical Problems

  • Two observers measure bearings to a target → find the distance using Law of Sines.
  • A ship travels on two bearings and known distances → find displacement using Law of Cosines.
  • Pilots correct for wind drift using vector addition and trig.

Astronomy Applications

Stellar Parallax

The distance to nearby stars is found using Earth's orbital diameter as a baseline and measuring the tiny angle of parallax — a direct application of the Law of Sines.

Orbital Calculations

Planetary positions, satellite orbits, and lunar distance calculations all involve oblique triangles.

Angular Separation

The angle between two celestial objects as seen from Earth.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: The Polar Coordinate System
summary_sections.content_html:
The Polar Coordinate System

A point is represented as (r, θ) where r is the distance from the origin (pole) and θ is the angle from the positive x-axis (polar axis).

Key Facts

  • Unlike Cartesian coordinates, polar representations are not unique: (r, θ) = (r, θ+2nπ) = (−r, θ+π).
  • Negative r means the point is in the opposite direction.
  • The origin has r = 0 for any θ.

flashcards.definition: The origin of the polar coordinate system.
The ray from the pole in the direction of the positive x-axis.
The directed distance from the pole to the point.
The angle measured counter-clockwise from the polar axis.
The same point has infinitely many polar representations: (r, θ+2nπ) and (−r, θ+π+2nπ).

summary_sections.content_html:
Graphing Polar Equations

Common Polar Curves

  • Circle: r = a (constant), r = a cos θ, r = a sin θ.
  • Cardioid: r = a ± a cos θ or r = a ± a sin θ.
  • Limaçon: r = a ± b cos θ (different a, b).
  • Rose: r = a cos(nθ) — n petals if n odd, 2n petals if n even.
  • Spiral: r = aθ (Archimedean).

summary_sections.content_html:
Complex Numbers in Polar Form

Any complex number z = a + bi can be written as:

z = r(cos θ + i sin θ) = r · cis θ

where r = |z| = √(a²+b²) is the modulus and θ = arg(z) is the argument.

  • z₁/z₂ = (r₁/r₂) · cis(θ₁−θ₂) — divide moduli, subtract arguments.

Adding sinusoidal waves with different phases is simplified by converting to phasors (complex exponentials) and adding the complex numbers.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Introduction to Vectors
summary_sections.content_html:
Introduction to Vectors

A vector has both magnitude and direction, unlike a scalar which has magnitude only.

Notation

  • Bold v or arrow notation v⃗.
  • Component form: v = ⟨v₁, v₂⟩ (2D) or ⟨v₁, v₂, v₃⟩ (3D).
  • Magnitude: |v| = √(v₁² + v₂²).

Position vs. Free Vectors

A free vector is defined only by magnitude and direction; it can be placed anywhere.

flashcards.definition: A quantity with both magnitude and direction, e.g., velocity, force.
A quantity with magnitude only, e.g., speed, temperature.
v = ⟨v₁, v₂⟩; the horizontal and vertical components.
|v| = √(v₁² + v₂²); the length of the vector.
A vector of magnitude 1; v̂ = v / |v|.

Vector Addition, Subtraction & Scalar Multiplication

Addition

u + v = ⟨u₁+v₁, u₂+v₂⟩. Geometrically: place tail of v at head of u (tip-to-tail).

Subtraction

u − v = ⟨u₁−v₁, u₂−v₂⟩. Geometrically: the vector from the tip of v to the tip of u.

Scalar Multiplication

cv = ⟨cv₁, cv₂⟩. Scales the length; reverses direction when c < 0.

summary_sections.content_html:
Introduction to the Cross Product

The cross product is defined in 3D: u × v produces a vector perpendicular to both u and v.

Formula

  • Direction: right-hand rule.
  • Anti-commutative: u × v = −(v × u).

summary_sections.content_html:
Trigonometric Proofs & Verification

Proving trig identities means showing the LHS equals the RHS through algebraic manipulation.

Strategies

  • Work on one side only (usually the more complex one).
  • Convert everything to sin and cos.
  • Factor, combine fractions, or multiply by a conjugate.
  • Use fundamental identities: Pythagorean, reciprocal, quotient.

flashcards.definition: An identity is true for ALL valid θ; an equation may be true only for specific θ.
Transform one side to match the other; never move terms across the equals sign.
Replace tan, sec, csc, cot with sin θ and cos θ expressions.
Multiply numerator & denominator by (1 + sinθ) or (1 − cosθ) to simplify.
Look for Pythagorean patterns, common factors, or difference of squares.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Trigonometric Substitution
summary_sections.content_html:
Trigonometric Substitution (Calculus Preview)

In calculus, trig substitution simplifies integrals involving √(a²−x²), √(a²+x²), or √(x²−a²).

The Three Cases

  • √(a²−x²): let x = a sinθ → √(a²−x²) = a cosθ.
  • √(a²+x²): let x = a tanθ → √(a²+x²) = a secθ.
  • √(x²−a²): let x = a secθ → √(x²−a²) = a tanθ.

flashcards.definition: Replacing x with a trig expression to eliminate a radical.
Use x = a sinθ; the radical becomes a cosθ.
Use x = a tanθ; the radical becomes a secθ.
Use x = a secθ; the radical becomes a tanθ.
After substituting, draw a right triangle to convert back to x.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Parametric Equations
summary_sections.content_html:
Parametric Equations

Instead of y = f(x), a curve is defined by x = f(t), y = g(t) with parameter t.

Key Skills

  • Graphing: Make a table of t → (x, y) and plot.
  • Eliminating the parameter: Express t in terms of x (or y) and substitute.
  • Direction: As t increases, trace the orientation of the curve.

Examples

x = cosθ, y = sinθ → unit circle. x = t², y = t → parabola x = y².
